text: The Da Vinci Surgical System is switched on, integrating its components using visible blue fiber optic cables. The patient cart lights illuminate, and the surgeon console checks the movement of hand controls. The patient cart arms undergo movement checks, and the touch screen displays a readiness message.

The touch screen displays a message to deploy the bool, showcasing its telestration feature that allows annotations to be sent to the surgeon console. The screen offers various options for controlling the console, including integrating previous surgical imaging like MRI or CT scans, viewing the endoscope, and utilizing Firefly technology for ICG fluorescence imaging. The mic and speaker facilitate communication between the assistant and the surgeon console.

The video processor unit is shown where the camera will be attached to the Da Vinci Surgical System's patient cart. The cart features four universal arms, each capable of holding 8mm instruments. Unlike the previous SI system, the camera can be placed in any of these arms. The arms have two sections, with the distal joints controlled by a port clutch button at the back. Pressing the instrument clutch button causes the LED to blink blue, indicating that the instrument or arm can be moved freely, though it does not control the proximal joints.

The distal set of joints on the robotic arm is controlled from the master console by the surgeon, while the proximal set of joints can be adjusted using the clutch button from the patient side to change port or arm positions. The patient clearance button allows the arm to rotate to prevent external clashing. The boom rotation button facilitates arm movement, simplifying docking in the latest generation model.

The boom rotation button is pressed, causing the boom to rotate. A green targeting light is visible, pointing towards the endoscope area, indicating proper alignment for docking. The boom can be adjusted for different surgical approaches, such as lower or upper abdominal surgeries, without altering the guard position. The touch screen and controls at the back of the system manage the movement of the patient cart, including throttle enable switch for forward or backward movement, and controls for adjusting boom position and height. Draping is performed, and the allotomy is selected based on the surgical site, with options for the patient cart's approach direction.

The Da Vinci XI surgical console is shown, featuring a stereo viewer for a three-dimensional high-definition view of the surgical field. The console includes hand controls for instrument manipulation, foot pedals for diathermy, energy, camera, clutch, and instrument swap functions, and a brake to stabilize the console's position.

The video shows white-colored instruments designed for use in human surgeries and red instruments intended for training purposes. The instruments feature a housing with discs and pulleys that translate the surgeon's hand movements into precise instrument tip movements, offering seven degrees of freedom. The force bipolar instrument is highlighted, demonstrating the fine wires and pulleys that control its movement. The instruments are connected to the patient cart via an adapter plate, receiving signals from the master console through fiber optic cables, allowing for precise control and a range of motion greater than the human hand.

The clip shows various robotic surgical instruments, including an eight-millimeter forged bipolar instrument with bipolar energy and a monopolar curved scissors used in hernia surgeries. The scissors have a curved design and seven degrees of freedom, making them versatile. The video also displays eight-millimeter cannulas used for port placement, featuring a fixed remote center to minimize trauma to the abdominal wall. The cannula seals are detachable, with options for air egress and ingress, accommodating both five and eight-millimeter instruments. The optical obturator is used for inserting trocar cards, and the patient cart is configured for docking with anatomical selection options.

The robotic system is configured for pelvic surgery, with the patient cart approaching from the patient's left side. The boom rotates and expands, positioning its four arms based on the pelvic anatomy. Laser lines guide the endoscope port alignment, and the patient cart is maneuvered accordingly. The boom then retracts to its original position for storage.

The robotic system rotates and positions itself for a pelvic procedure, with its arms opening up. The patient cart directs the laser light towards the endoscope port. Dr. Tushar plugs the endoscope into the vision card, aligning the green laser light precisely at the endoscope position. The endoscope is then inserted, and any of the robotic arms can be used for the camera.

Dr. Pishad uses arm two and arm three for the procedure, while arm four is not utilized. The camera port is docked, and the endoscope is installed for targeting. The illuminator is switched on, and the endoscope is pointed at the target anatomy. The targeting button is pressed, causing the arms to rotate and align with the target anatomy. Dr. Tushar docks arm three, and arm two is docked, completing the setup.

The robotic instruments are placed in position on the docked arms, with the right instrument identified as a middle driver and the left as a Maryland bipolar. The surgeon uses the instrument clutch to bring the instruments into view under the camera. The American Grasper and needle holder are visible as Dr. Tushar positions them. The surgeon sits at the master console, adjusting ergonomics and using hand controls to manipulate the instruments, ensuring precise movements within the surgical field. The camera paddle is used to zoom and rotate the camera for better visualization.

The surgeon uses the camera paddle to zoom in and out, providing a detailed view of the surgical field. The robotic instruments are manipulated with precision, showcasing their ability to rotate in multiple directions. The pulleys and fibers visibly translate hand movements into the instrument tips, enhancing control. Diathermy panels are available on both sides for energy application, and foot pedals are used to control the robotic arms and camera movements, allowing for precise adjustments and energy application.

Dr. Tushar removes the instrument and the bipolar device by pressing release levers on the housing. The camera is also removed, and the illuminator is switched off. The surgical arms are undocked by pressing clips, and the system is moved away from the patient, ready for storage.
